title: Chapter 15: The Watching
content: The muslin is cool against my skin.

Not cold — cool. The way river water is cool in late summer, when the sun has warmed the surface but the current underneath still carries the memory of mountains. That kind of cool. The kind that awakens without shocking.

I've pinned the bodice in three places along the princess seam and I'm working on the fourth — the curve under the arm, where the fabric has to lie flat against the ribcage but still allow movement, still allow breath. This is the part that always takes the longest. The geometry of the living body. You can draft it on paper a hundred times, but fabric against flesh is its own country, with its own laws, and no mannequin — no matter how well-calibrated — can account for the way a body shifts when it inhales.
So I am the form tonight.

The muslin wraps around my torso, pinned at the shoulder, hanging loose at the hip where I haven't gotten to the skirt yet. I've tucked the excess at the side seam with two pins and there's a third pin between my lips, copper-tasting and warm from my breath, waiting for the moment I need it. My fingers know where it goes before my mind does — they reach for the fabric at the same time my teeth release the pin, and by the time the thought catches up, the pin is seated, the muslin is smooth, and I'm reaching for another from the pincushion on the table.

The studio is dark except for the single desk lamp I've angled toward the mirror. The LED bulb reads warm — 2700 Kelvin, the color of candlelight if candlelight were steady and didn't smoke. It casts a circle of amber across the cutting table, the dress form in the corner draped in black wool, the stack of pattern weights my mother gave me — brass, octagonal, heavy in the palm. Beyond that circle, the studio exists in suggestion only: shapes in darkness, the outline of my industrial machine, the silhouette of the bolt rack.
I didn't turn on the overheads. I never do when I'm working like this. The darkness isn't an absence — it's a perimeter. It says: this space is only for the work. This light is only for the making.

The building's ventilation hums through the walls. Low and constant, a drone in B-flat that I've stopped hearing in the way you stop hearing your own heartbeat. But tonight I notice it, because for once it's louder than the other hum — the one that lives under my skin, the one that rises around Rowan like a tide. Tonight the external noise fills the space where the internal one usually sits, and the swap is almost comforting. Almost. Like substituting one kind of quiet for another.

I turn toward the mirror to check the drape at the waist, and I see myself in the half-made garment — muslin skin over my skin, pins catching the light like small bright stitches in the air. The bust point is right. The waist suppression is right. I've been doing this for four hundred years and I still feel a small, clean thrill when a line falls exactly where I intended it to. Not pride. Something humbler than that. Gratitude, maybe. That the body and the fabric agreed.
I reach for another pin.
I don't hear him arrive.

I don't hear the door push wider on its hinges, or the soft shift of his weight on the old floorboards, or the way the air in the room redistributes itself around a second body. I'm deep in the work — that state where the hands know and the mind follows and the self becomes a conduit rather than a source. I'm adjusting the ease at the back shoulder, my arm twisted behind me, pin between my teeth again, and I'm frowning at my reflection and the frown is the kind that means I'm not thinking about anything except the angle of the shoulder line.
And then I feel it.

Not the hum. Something else. The particular quality of silence that arrives when someone is watching you — the way the air thickens, the way the back of your neck registers attention before your eyes do. I know that silence. I've known it for centuries.
I turn.
Rowan is standing in the doorway.

Not in the doorway. Just past it. One step inside my studio, one hand still on the doorframe like he might need it to steady himself, or like he might need it to leave. The door is ajar behind him — I left it that way, a violation of every rule I set about space, about boundaries, about the sanctity of the closed door. I was going to close it. I was going to close it after I pinned the shoulder. I forgot.
He's not moving.

He's standing in the amber light from the hallway and his face is — I don't have a word for what his face is. Undone, maybe. The way a face looks when it's forgotten to perform. His mouth is slightly open and his eyes are fixed on me with an intensity that has nothing predatory in it and everything devotional. He's watching the way you watch something you're afraid will disappear if you blink. The way you watch a stained glass window when the sun hits it at precisely the right angle and the whole wall becomes a prayer.
He's watching me like I'm something sacred.
The pins fall from my lips. They hit the floor — three of them, a bright tinny clatter that sounds almost comically loud in the silence, like a spell breaking in a fairy tale.
"You're supposed to call first," I say.

My voice comes out steady. I'm grateful for that. I'm less grateful for the way my hand has found the muslin at my hip and gripped it, bunching the fabric in my fist, like I need something to hold onto.
"I know." Rowan's voice is low. Rough at the edges, like it's been scraped against something. "I was walking past and I saw the light."
He doesn't say which light — the lamp through the half-open door, or something else. Something less explainable.

He hasn't moved from the doorway. He's giving me the distance even though he's already crossed the threshold. Even though he's already in my space. Even though the door is ajar and I left it that way and we both know what that means, or what it could mean, if either of us were willing to say it.
"I've never watched anyone work before," he says. "Not like this."
"Not like what?"
He pauses. His jaw shifts — the muscle there, the one that tightens when he's choosing his words with unusual care.
"Like it's the same as breathing."

The sentence lands in my chest. Not in the hum. Deeper than that. In the place where the four hundred years live, the accumulation of centuries that sometimes feels like weight and sometimes feels like wings. He said it simply. Like it was self-evident. Like he looked at me working and saw what I've always known but have never had anyone see: that the making is not something I do. It is something I am.
He steps closer.

The movement is slow, deliberate — the way you approach something wild, something that might startle. Or the way you approach something you've been walking toward for a long time and you want the arriving to matter.

The desk lamp catches the emerald ring on his right hand. The stone flares — a brief green fire in the amber light — and then darkens as his hand falls back to his side. His eyes don't flash. They're dark and steady and intent, and they're looking at me the way he looked at me on the dance floor at the benefit, when the music found the frequency of the hum and I thought I would shake apart from the inside. Like he's trying to solve a problem that has no solution. Like the problem is me, and the not-solving is the point.

I should tell him to leave. I should close the door and turn back to the mirror and finish the shoulder line. I should protect the perimeter I drew with the darkness and the single lamp and the silence.
I don't tell him to leave.

He's close enough now that I can smell him — cedar and something green, like crushed leaves after rain, like a forest floor. The scent drifts across the boundary of my workspace and settles into the warm lamp-air of the studio, mixing with the familiar smell of muslin and metal and the faintly chemical sweetness of the pattern spray I used earlier.
He reaches out.

His hand moves slowly enough that I could stop him. Could step back. Could say his name in the voice that means *don't*. I don't do any of these things. I stand inside the half-made dress and I watch his hand approach, and the hum — the hum rises, but only a little. A murmur. The sound of water before it reaches the boil. The sound of a held breath.
His fingers touch the muslin at my waist.
Not my skin. The fabric.

His hand settles against the pinned seam, right where the princess line curves into the waist, and his fingers trace the pin line — three pins in a row, each one catching the lamplight as his fingertip passes over it. The touch is light. Precise. A seamstress's touch, almost. The touch of someone who understands the architecture of a garment, or who is trying to.
"This is extraordinary," he says.

His voice is rough. It scrapes on the word *extraordinary* like the syllables are too large for his throat. And I know — the way I know where a pin goes before my mind catches up — that he's not talking about the dress. He's talking about what the dress is doing. What my body is doing inside it. What his hand on the fabric over my waist is doing to the space between us, which is no space at all.
I hold very still.

The muslin is thin enough that I feel the warmth of his hand — not the skin of his hand, but the heat of it, radiating through the cotton, transferring across the boundary of fabric into the skin beneath. It's the most indirect touch I have ever felt and the most intimate, because the fabric doesn't protect me from it. The fabric transmits it. The fabric, which is my element, my armor, my language — the fabric takes his heat and delivers it to my body like a message in code. Like a whispered confession.
The hum stirs. Low. Patient. It doesn't roar. It waits.
"You're breaking my rule," I say.
My voice is not steady anymore. It's careful. I'm placing each word like a pin, trying to hold a line that I'm not sure I want to hold.
"I'm touching the garment," he says. "Not you."
His eyes meet mine.
They're dark and close and they say: same thing. They say: we both know it's the same thing. They say: I found the loophole in your rule and I'm standing in it and I'm not moving and neither are you.

His fingers are still tracing the pin line. Back and forth — a small, slow arc from the bottom pin to the top. The muslin moves with his touch, shifting against my skin, and every micro-movement registers like a language I've spoken for centuries. Fabric against flesh. His hand against the fabric. The two sensations blurring into one.

This is a touch that isn't a touch. This is a boundary that isn't a boundary. This is the door I left ajar and the man who walked through it and the rule I made that was never going to hold, not against this, not against the way he's looking at me like the garment and the body are the same country and he's mapping the border between them with his fingertips.
I should step back. I should take his wrist and move his hand and say *this is my studio and my work and my body and you can't have all three.*
I don't move.

The ventilation hums. The lamp hums. The hum hums, low and patient, and I stand inside the half-made dress and let his hand rest on the fabric over my waist and I feel the warmth of him seeping through the muslin like dye into silk — slow, inevitable, permanent.
He steps back.

The moment breaks like a held breath. One second his warmth is against my waist and the next there is only air — cool air, studio air, the space where his hand was now empty and the muslin settling back against my skin, still carrying the ghost of his temperature.
"Goodnight, Orlando," he says.

His voice is formal. He's putting the walls back up. I can see him doing it — the way his shoulders square, the way his hand returns to his side, the way his face recalibrates from undone to composed. He's giving me the distance again. He's closing the door he walked through. He's being the version of himself that respects rules and spaces and the things I said I needed.
"Goodnight," I say.
The word feels small. Inadequate. Like handing someone a thimble when they need an ocean.

He turns. He walks toward the door. His hand touches the frame again, the same hand that was on my waist — I can see the emerald ring, dark now in the amber light, no bright flare, just the shape of it. His fingers curl around the wood.
He pauses.
"For what it's worth," he says, without turning around, "I think you were born to do this. I don't know how I know. But I know."

Then he's gone. The door stays ajar. The hallway light makes an amber rectangle on my floor and then goes dark — he must have hit the switch on his way out. The building's ventilation resumes its drone in B-flat. The studio is dark except for the lamp, and the lamp is only light enough for the work, and the work is not done.
I stand in the half-made dress.

The pins I dropped are still on the floor — three of them, scattered like small bright stars where they fell from my lips. I should pick them up. I should close the door. I should finish the shoulder line and press the side seam and go home.
I lift my hand.

I press it against the muslin at my waist — over the same pin line his fingers traced, in the exact place his hand rested. The fabric is cool again. River-water cool. The warmth has faded, is fading still, a memory of heat that my skin is already losing to the studio air.

But I can feel it. The trace of it. The way you can feel a hand on your shoulder after it's been removed — the phantom weight, the echo of touch, the body's stubborn insistence that *something was here, something was real, something pressed against me and meant it.*
The muslin is just muslin. The pins are just pins. The garment is just a garment.

But he touched the garment like it was me. And I felt it through the fabric like it was skin. And the boundary we drew — the careful, articulate, sensible boundary — is dissolving between us like salt in water, like dye in silk, and neither of us is trying to save it.
Neither of us wants to.
I stand in the half-made dress and I don't pick up the pins and I don't close the door, and the hum, patient and low, murmurs on.
